title: Gaussian Processes for Shuffled Regression
abstract: Shuffled regression is the problem of learning regression functions from shuffled data where the correspondence between the input features and target response is unknown. This paper proposes a probabilistic model for shuffled regression called Gaussian Process Shuffled Regression (GPSR). By introducing Gaussian processes as a prior of regression functions in function space via the kernel function, GPSR can express a wide variety of functions in a nonparametric manner while quantifying the uncertainty of the prediction. By adopting the Bayesian evidence maximization framework and a theoretical analysis of the connection between the marginal likelihood/predictive distribution of GPSR and that of standard Gaussian process regression (GPR), we derive an easy-to-implement inference algorithm for GPSR that iteratively applies GPR and updates the input-output correspondence. To reduce computation costs and obtain closed-form solutions for correspondence updates, we also develop a sparse approximate variant of GPSR using its weight space formulation, which can be seen as Bayesian shuffled linear regression with random Fourier features. Experiments on benchmark datasets confirm the effectiveness of our GPSR proposal.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper introduces a novel Gaussian Process-based framework for shuffled regression, termed Gaussian Process Shuffled Regression (GPSR), along with its sparse variant, SS-GPSR. The goal of shuffled regression is to learn a regression function from "shuffled data" where the correspondence between input features and target responses is unknown. GPSR leverages Gaussian Processes (GP) as a functional prior, employing kernel functions for nonparametric modeling and providing principled quantification of predictive uncertainty. The authors establish the theoretical equivalence between the marginal likelihood and predictive distribution of GPSR and those of standard Gaussian Process Regression (GPR). Building on this, they develop an alternating optimization algorithm that iteratively updates kernel hyperparameters (via standard GPR subroutines) and input-output correspondences (by solving a Quadratic Assignment Problem, QAP). To improve computational efficiency, the authors further propose SS-GPSR, which approximates GPSR using Random Fourier Features (RFF) to map the model into weight space, resulting in a Bayesian Shuffled Linear Regression (BSLR) formulation. This enables closed-form updates of the correspondence via sorting operations. Empirical results on multiple UCI datasets demonstrate that GPSR and SS-GPSR outperform existing shuffled regression approaches, particularly in modeling nonlinear relationships and quantifying uncertainty.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:

(1) The proposed method is rigorously designed with clear theoretical underpinnings. In particular, Propositions 4.2 and 4.3 establish a theoretical equivalence between GPSR and standard GPR, and the experimental evaluation is comprehensive, involving multiple datasets and varying set sizes.

(2) The work is the first to introduce Gaussian Processes into the shuffled regression setting and further develops an efficient sparse variant, SS-GPSR.

(3) Shuffled regression has practical relevance in scenarios involving privacy-preserving data (e.g., medical records) and multi-source data integration. The adoption of nonparametric modeling enhances the expressive capacity of existing approaches in these settings.

Weaknesses:

(1) SS-GPSR updates the input-output correspondence via a sorting operation (§5.2), which is optimal only under the assumption of a strict rank alignment between the predicted means v_i and responses y_i. The paper does not analyze the robustness of this assumption under noise or model misspecification, nor does it quantify the impact of sorting errors on regression performance. Notably, the derivation in Appendix F omits consideration of distributional shifts in the noise.

(2) The experiments are limited to low-dimensional UCI datasets (with a maximum feature dimension D=13), and the method's robustness in high-dimensional settings (e.g., image embeddings) or under complex noise structures (e.g., heteroscedastic noise) remains untested. Moreover, SS-GPSR exhibits noticeable performance variance at L=16 in Table 3 (e.g., MPG dataset shows a standard deviation of 3.03 in MSE), but the source of this instability is not discussed.

(3) Implementation details of the simulated annealing algorithm in Appendix C are incomplete—for example, the temperature schedule T and the acceptance probability q are unspecified. Furthermore, the paper does not explain how the QAP solver avoids convergence to suboptimal local minima (§4.2).

(4) Although SS-GPSR is claimed to reduce computational complexity, the paper does not provide runtime comparisons with existing baselines (e.g., SDR), nor does it analyze the joint impact of the RFF dimension H and the set size L on computational cost (§6).

QUESTIONS:
The authors are encouraged to address the following critical issues through additional analysis or experiments, as they directly impact the paper’s overall evaluation:

(1) SS-GPSR updates the correspondence matrix $\Pi_i$ via sorting of the predicted means $v_i$ and observed responses $y_i$ (\S5.2). However, when the underlying regression function is non-monotonic or the noise level is high, this procedure may yield incorrect pairings. A theoretical analysis is needed to specify the optimality conditions for sorting---e.g., under what smoothness or monotonicity assumptions Proposition 5.1 holds. In addition, synthetic experiments should be conducted to quantify how the sorting error rate and regression MSE vary with increasing noise levels, similar to Figure 2.

(2) Current experiments are limited to low-dimensional tabular datasets. The effectiveness of GPSR and SS-GPSR in higher-dimensional feature spaces (e.g., $D > 100$) or under non-stationary conditions (e.g., covariate shift) remains unexplored. Please include experiments on at least one high-dimensional dataset (e.g., CIFAR-10 image embeddings) or temporally shuffled sensor data collected from heterogeneous devices. Comparative analysis with SDR in these settings would strengthen the empirical claims.

(3) While SS-GPSR is claimed to be more computationally efficient than GPSR, no quantitative comparisons of runtime or memory usage are provided. Please include such metrics and analyze the computational trade-offs, particularly contrasting the theoretical complexities $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ (GPSR) and $\mathcal{O}(H^3)$ (SS-GPSR).

(4) In GPSR, the QAP subproblem is solved via simulated annealing (\S4.2), a stochastic optimization approach that lacks direct theoretical grounding in Bayesian inference. This introduces a potential disconnect with the marginal likelihood maximization framework. Please discuss how simulated annealing affects convergence and posterior estimation—possibly in comparison with MCMC methods~\cite{ref20}—or provide a theoretical or empirical guarantee of the monotonic increase of the marginal likelihood $\mathcal{L}_{\text{GPSR}}$ during the iterative process.

LIMITATIONS:
While the authors briefly acknowledge certain limitations and social implications in the conclusion section, several critical aspects require further elaboration:

(1) GPSR assumes the underlying regression function follows a Gaussian Process (GP) prior, yet the paper does not discuss potential failure cases when this assumption is violated—for example, in the presence of heavy-tailed or non-Gaussian data distributions. It is recommended to include a synthetic experiment in the appendix that compares GPSR with quantile-based shuffled regression methods when the ground-truth function f deviates significantly from the GP prior.

(2) SS-GPSR relies on Random Fourier Features (RFF), which inherently assume a stationary (translation-invariant) kernel (§3). However, the applicability of the method to non-stationary kernels is not examined, such as deep kernels [42]. The paper should clarify this kernel restriction and discuss possible extensions or limitations regarding kernel choice.

(3) Although the conclusion mentions that prediction performance should be evaluated with caution, it does not address the concrete risks of incorrect input-output alignment, such as patient-label mismatches in medical records. It would be beneficial to discuss whether a confidence-based rejection mechanism is necessary for privacy-sensitive or high-risk applications, e.g., imposing an uncertainty threshold to discard low-confidence predictions.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper proposes Gaussian Process Shuffled Regression (GPSR) and its scalable variant SS-GPSR for the problem of learning regression functions from shuffled data, where the input–output correspondence is unknown. The authors formulate a Bayesian evidence maximization framework in which both the regression function and permutation matrix are inferred. GPSR uses a Gaussian process prior, and inference alternates between Gaussian process regression and solving a quadratic assignment problem. To address the scalability challenges of GPSR, the authors develop SS-GPSR, a sparse approximation based on random Fourier features and a weight-space formulation. A key technical insight is the introduction of a latent variable $w$, which yields a marginal likelihood that matches that of GPSR at optimality but depends only linearly on the permutation matrix, enabling efficient updates via sorting. Experimental comparisons against baselines show that SS-GPSR achieves competitive accuracy with improved scalability.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
#### **Strengths**

* The paper presents a clear, principled formulation of shuffled regression using Gaussian processes and evidence maximization.
* The derivation of SS-GPSR is elegant, with the introduction of the auxiliary variable $w$ allowing efficient optimization through a linear dependence on the permutation matrix.
* The paper is clearly written and accessible, even through dense derivations.
* The closed-form inference and uncertainty quantification offered by the GP-based models set them apart from purely neural alternatives.

#### **Weaknesses**

* While the mathematical development is thorough, the core approach ultimately reduces to kernel ridge regression with a permutation matrix estimated via alternating optimization. From this perspective, the methodological contribution may feel incremental, though well-executed.
* Experiments are relatively limited in scale and do not fully evaluate how SS-GPSR behaves as dataset size or set size $L$ increases.
* SS-GPSR does not optimize kernel hyperparameters ($\theta$), and the impact of this design choice on performance is not explored.
* No runtime comparisons are provided, even though efficiency is one of the main selling points of SS-GPSR.

QUESTIONS:
1. **Interaction of kernel and permutation optimization**
   In Figure 2, GPSR is applied to a toy sine wave task. Can you provide insight into how optimization of the kernel hyperparameters and the permutation matrix interact? For example, if the data is highly shuffled, will the GP favor a short length scale and overfit? Could this lead to poor local optima?

2. **Lack of kernel optimization in SS-GPSR**
   SS-GPSR does not optimize the kernel hyperparameters $\theta$. Is this the main price paid for scalability? In settings where tuning $\theta$ is important for performance, how does this limitation affect results?

3. **Convergence behavior of alternating updates**
   Since $w$ and $\Pi$ are each optimized globally in SS-GPSR, is there any convergence guarantee for the alternating procedure? Could the method oscillate between solutions with the same likelihood? Empirical observations or theoretical discussion here would be helpful.

4. **Runtime and scalability experiments**
   A central claim is that SS-GPSR is more efficient than GPSR and SDR. To support this, it would be helpful to include runtime-to-convergence plots for varying $L$, and wall-clock comparisons across methods.

5. **Larger-scale experiments**
   Current experiments are limited to relatively small datasets. If feasible, then I think evaluating performance at larger $N$ and especially larger $L$ would better illustrate the scalability of SS-GPSR and its competitiveness with neural methods like SDR.


If the authors can provide meaningful insight into the optimization dynamics (especially around kernel/permutation interactions), clarify the limitations of not tuning $\theta$ in SS-GPSR, and supply runtime or larger-scale evaluations, I would be inclined to raise my score.

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper introduces Gaussian Process Shuffled Regression (GPSR), a probabilistic model for learning regression functions when input-output correspondences are unknown, a setting referred to as *shuffled regression*. GPSR places Gaussian process priors over functions in the input space and iteratively recovers the correspondence using a Bayesian evidence maximisation framework. To address computational scalability, the authors propose a sparse variant (SS-GPSR) using random Fourier features, enabling closed-form correspondence updates via sorting. Theoretical connections to standard GPR and Bayesian linear models are established, leading to a principled and efficient inference scheme. Empirical results on benchmark datasets provide comparisons between (SS-)GPSR, classical shuffled regression methods (linear and deep shuffled regression), and oracle baselines using unshuffled data.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
The article is well-written and didactic, making it easy to follow throughout successive derivations. All involved methods are carefully detailed and re-explained in the Appendix for the most basic ones. The problem being addressed, extending GP regression to the case of shuffled measurements, is clearly positioned within existing literature. As a GP expert, I was unaware of this unusual *shuffled* context, which made it interesting to discover. Although the proposed method mainly builds on existing ideas, they are combined in a sensible way. In particular, Random Fourier Features (RFF) tackles both GP scalability and the quadratic assignment problem (QAP) at the same time, which is an elegant proposal. The experimental setting is convincing and effectively highlights the key properties of the methods (except for two important aspects, which are detailed in the *Questions* section). 

Overall, I believe that this article meets the threshold for publication and constitutes an incremental yet valuable contribution to both the GP and the shuffled regression literatures.

QUESTIONS:
The paper carefully describes and discusses the key aspects we could expect in this framework. I only have two remaining questions after reading: 

- Could you provide an empirical comparison of training/prediction times for all methods involved in the experiments? As SLR is used as initialisation in GPSR, I suspect it's orders of magnitude quicker (which is fine, but it would be valuable to know the exact time/accuracy tradeoff). For problems with large values of L, we can observe that the difference between (SS-)GPSR and SLR decreases; therefore, discussing this aspect may be more fair and would provide more nuanced takeaways to readers. 

- Although it is a key desirable property of GP-based frameworks, no evaluation of the uncertainty quantification (UQ) is provided. I understand that competing methods do not provide off-the-shelf uncertainty measures for comparison. However, it would be valuable at least to evaluate whether UQ is well-calibrated for (SS-)GPSR, for instance, by computing the number of testing points effectively lying within a credible interval. If a correct UQ is preserved in the (SS-)GPSR framework, it would be a significant benefit compared with existing approaches, and this feature would deserve to be highlighted accordingly. On the other hand, if UQ is somewhat damaged during the inference procedure, it could be presented as a natural and valuable path for future work, which could be mentioned in the conclusion.

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper studies the shuffled regression problem from a Gaussian Process (GP) perspective. By introducing the GP, they additionally provide the uncertainty measure while improving the SOTA performance. Moreover, to make the method scalable, they introduce the spectral representation of the kernel to reduce the time complexity and memory consumption.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:

1. The paper clearly presents the idea of Gaussian Process Shuffled Regression (GPSR) and is overall well-written and easy to follow.
2. It is the first to introduce Gaussian Processes (GPs) to the shuffled regression setting, addressing a novel and relevant problem.
3. A scalable variant, BSLR, is proposed to address the computational limitations of GPSR.
4. The paper demonstrates that GPSR and BSLR produce equivalent results under exact spectral approximation of the kernel, providing theoretical justification for the scalable method.
5. The proposed method is evaluated on four real-world datasets and consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art baselines.

Weaknesses:
1. Although the optimization details of GPSR are clearly described, the derivation or computation of the feature map $\phi$ in BSLR is omitted, which negatively affects the clarity and completeness of the scalable method. It would also be helpful to include error bounds for the spectral approximation to better understand the conditions under which BSLR remains reliable.
2. The SOTA baseline SDR is claimed to be a deep method, yet the implementation in the experiments uses only a single hidden layer. This may raise fairness concerns in the experimental comparison.
3. The paper does not report training or inference time, despite the known scalability challenges of GP-based methods.
4. There is no empirical scalability analysis. It would be valuable to see how the proposed method scales in terms of runtime, memory, and performance with increasing numbers of samples ($N$) or spectral features ($M$).
5. All experimental datasets are low-dimensional, where a spectral dimension $H=100$ suffices. However, it remains unclear how $H$ needs to scale with higher-dimensional input, which is important for understanding the general applicability of the method.

I would be inclined to raise my score if the authors address the following key concerns: (1) ensuring a fairer comparison by using deeper architectures for the SDR baseline as claimed; (2) reporting training and inference time as well as memory consumption; and (3) providing a clearer analysis or empirical evidence regarding the scalability of the proposed method, particularly in high-dimensional or large-scale settings.

QUESTIONS:
The questions are listed in the weaknesses.

Besides, I wonder what the computational complexity is with the spectral decomposition method. The authors only provided the memory complexity, but the computation complexity is missing.

Lastly, it seems that a deep version of the proposed method could be developed by replacing the kernel with a deep kernel, following the approach of Wilson et al. [1]. This extension may further boost performance and help adapt the method to high-dimensional or more complex data distributions. Exploring this direction, or at least discussing its feasibility, would enrich the contribution and position the work more clearly within the broader GP literature.

[1] Wilson, Andrew Gordon, et al. "Deep kernel learning." Artificial intelligence and statistics. PMLR, 2016.

LIMITATIONS:
The scalability of the proposed method remains uncertain when applied to complex, high-dimensional datasets. In particular, it is unclear how the number of spectral features ($H$) must grow and how computational cost scales in such settings.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (poster)

COMMENT:
The authors propose a GP-based model for shuffled regression, i.e. regression in which sets of data points map to sets of responses, but the correspondence is not known in advance.  Existing methods for the problem include a linear regression approach and a neural network based approach; the authors propose a GP together with a permutation chosen by evidence maximization.  In particular, they propose two methods: one that uses a function space perspective and computes the permutations via a (hard) quadratic assignment problem; and one that uses Bayesian linear regression with random Fourier features and computes the permutations via a (much simpler) linear assignment problem.  The accuracy of the new methods is compared to the existing methods on a number of benchmarks.

I would summarize the paper as having three main pieces:

- The idea of using a GP to solve a shuffled regression problem at all (and using an evidence maximization approach, though this is done in a pretty standard way)
- The idea of converting to a feature-vector formulation and computing the permutation by a linear assignment problem.
- The idea of integrating all this with a low-rank approximation to the kernel and solving as a weight space form / regularized least squares problem.

The first two pieces seem to be new; the third piece is standard.  The exposition lumps the second two pieces together (though one could use a Cholesky factorization of the kernel matrix to get to a linear assignment problem, so the use of a low-rank approximation is not really fundamental here) and only considers a single flavor of low-rank approximation via random Fourier features (though an SVD, Nystrom approximation, or other approach would work without any real modification).

The paper is well-written, and the authors generally liked the idea.  However, there were two broadly-shared concerns.  First, several reviewers commented that the input space was rather low-dimensional in all the examples, and asked what would happen for higher-dimensional problems.  The authors replied that most of the shuffled regression literature deals with fairly low-dimensional inputs (unlike in more general supervised learning settings), and asked that this be taken into account.  This response seemed to basically satisfy the reviewers (or at least it did not prompt further discussion).  Second, the reviewers all pointed out that despite the claim that one selling point of the methods is scalability -- particularly for the random-feature-based method -- there are no timing experiments.  The authors essentially replied that the new algorithms use known building blocks with known performance, that one can reason about the performance in terms of those building blocks, and that "investigating existing GPR and BLR algorithms [...] is not the essential contribution of this study."  The reviewers again did not comment further on this, though I still find the lack of concrete timing numbers somewhat unsatisfactory.

Two other significant points came up in the reviews and were addressed by the authors.  First, two reviewers (fcj5 and aYMq) pointed out that the SS-GPSR method as described seems to restrict one to stationary kernels; the authors replied that this is not a fundamental restriction, and it will be addressed briefly in the camera-ready.  Second, reviewer TTAf mentioned that no uncertainty quantification was actually shown in the paper, though this is one of the benefits of using a GP-based method.  The authors replied that they had done some preliminary experiments in response to this question (which they shared); the results were not entirely satisfactory, but they said they would say more in the camera ready.

The key reasons to accept would be that this is a new approach to an interesting class or problems (shuffled regression), and it seems to achieve pretty good accuracy compared to competitors.  The key shortcoming is a lack of experimental study related to performance, though I also think separately treating the conversion to a linear assignment problem and the approximation via a low-rank method (of which RFF is just one example) might clarify some of the problems and help with reader questions about applicability to non-stationary kernels similar to what the reviewers have.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
